title: AlexNet & the ImageNet Shock
caption: AlexNet's eight-layer convolutional architecture, trained on two GPUs, that halved the ImageNet error rate in 2012.

overview.text: At the 2012 ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge, Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever and Geoffrey Hinton entered a deep convolutional neural network — later called AlexNet — that achieved a top-5 error of 15.3%, compared to 26.2% for the next-best entry. The gap was so large it shocked the computer-vision community and triggered a wholesale shift toward deep learning. AlexNet used ReLU activations, dropout regularisation, and GPU training — techniques that became standard across the field.

deep.text: AlexNet stacked five convolutional layers and three fully-connected layers, trained on 1.2 million labelled images across two NVIDIA GTX 580 GPUs. ReLU activations replaced saturating sigmoids, dramatically speeding convergence. Dropout — randomly zeroing half the hidden units during training — prevented co-adaptation and acted as an ensemble of exponentially many subnetworks, reducing overfitting without extra data.

challenge.prompt: What was AlexNet's top-5 error rate on ImageNet 2012? (The runner-up scored about 26%)

feedback.correct: Right — 15.3% top-5 error, versus 26.2% for the next competitor. The gap was the real story.
feedback.incorrect: AlexNet scored 15.3% top-5 error rate, nearly 11 percentage points better than the runner-up at 26.2%.

sources.title: ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
sources.author: A. Krizhevsky, I. Sutskever & G. E. Hinton
sources.publisher: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 25 (NeurIPS)
